speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of the interview with Hyme Hipsman. My name is Amy Tarr. Mr. Hipsman, what happened in Wlodawa in 1941, when you returned from your job as a shepherd?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened then before-- when I went to be a shepherd, they-- they gave us-- they took me off of the ration cards for the whole summer. Then when I came back, they had to-- they had to put me back on the ration cards. But all of a sudden, the Germans, the SD, somebody told the SD there's something about it. And the SD, it was, I would say, on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe 10 people of us. But they did the same thing. And they called us to the SD, and I went up there. And he asked me if I have [NON-ENGLISH], lice. I said, I haven't got it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he didn't question me too much. He said, you go home, and bring me a $50-- 50 zlotys penalty. And that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was working for the-- with a tailor. So I went to the tailor, and I told him, give me 50 zlotys, because I have to pay a fine there. So he gave me the 50 zlotys, and I went and I paid. And I came back, and I was sitting down and doing my work. All of a sudden, my mother runs with a cry. And she cries and cries. And before-- before she opened the door, she said, they killed everybody there. I didn't-- when I left-- when I paid him the 50--the 50 zlotys-- I didn't know what happened to the other ones.   I thought they were going to do the same thing-- they give them a fine, and they'll go back. And she came in with a cry that they killed everybody. So it looked to me that me-- there was another young boy there-- he us go for the 50 zlotys fine. And he took other rest by the cemetery-- it was maybe not too far from the-- from the building there-- and he took them, and shoot the other-- the other eight people. That's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother didn't know at first?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't know because when I came back to the tailor for the 50 zlotys, I didn't went to-- to my mother give me money. And she didn't know. She didn't know that what happened, or why I'm alive, or what, until she came to me and I told her. So she quieted down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hipsman, what happened in Wlodawa in 1943? It had been a ghetto since 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 1943 was already Judenrein-- it means clean up Jews. And it was the last-- the last thing. So at the time, I worked for Wagenberg. And we used to go-- we used to wake up every morning with the-- with the bell. The bell was ringing, we knew we had to come and concentrate and go from there to work. And here, I hear the bell rings and rings and rings, and no stop to  it. And I look out of the window, and I see people are running. So I said, oh, I don't like that. It's something fishy there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went out, and I run with the people. And they went in a hiding place, and I went in with them the hiding place. And I stayed for three days in the hiding place. And in the night time, we used to go out. And some other people used to come out and see what's going out. Usually, after a thing like that, always after they took the people and killed them, you could go out and be free again, you know? In here, you couldn't go out. So they said that the only thing that Wagenberg told them, that this is it-- this is Judenrein. We cannot live in the city anymore. The only way we can go is to go in the woods-- survive in the woods. So I think that was Wagenberg, gave some people a hint what to do. The bells? That was his hint-- is that what you mean? That Wagenberg rang the bell over an over to give you warning?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no-- when he rang-- ring the bell, he want everybody to fake them, to come out, and to stay there, and to wait. And then the Germans came to take him away. Wagenberg didn't-- I guess they told him to do it, you know, but he didn't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't a bad guy, you know? He was a pretty good-- pretty good German, you know. So that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then I didn't went to the woods. We went-- it was like a big farm there-- maybe 10 kilometers. They named it Adampol.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adampol, A A-D-A-P-O-L-- Adampol. And somehow, the Germans allowed the rest of the Jews that had survived, they should come there and gathering together again. And from then, pick them up again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe two-- I would say maybe a month. And all-- and from there, I went to look for my mother. I thought she's still alive. I had to go back at nighttime to the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went with another-- another friend of mine. We went to the city and-- to look for my mother at nighttime. So when I came in, in the city, and I opened up-- there was a cellar there-- I opened up the cellar. Usually, I knew that she's going to be there. So I saw people in the basement-- in the cellar. And I asked-- I called my mother's name. And they said my mother left the night before. And they didn't know where she left, or what she left. And I never saw my mother again.

speaker: interviewee
text: But those people, what I saw them there, they were all already in that-- in that basement, they were already, I would say, maybe three weeks. And they ask me, what was the story? What-- what should we do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told them, listen, the only-- you cannot go out, because it's Judenrein. You go out, they'll take you. Or they will kill you on the spot, or send you to Sobibor. So I said to them that the only way, if you want to survive, you know, better to take in the woods. So-- and then, I left them. They went out, and they went in the woods. And I took back to go back to Adampol. But when we came to the city, I'm-- I-- I told my friend that we're going to meet in that place, just to go back together. And all of a sudden, I went back to the place, and he came back to the place. And from one place jumped out somebody, and they said to us, halt! We should stay. But I knew Wlodawa very well-- all the places. And I just pulled back and ran, right and left, right and left. And then I disappeared from him. But the other fellow, I never saw him. I guess they killed him, the other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back during the night to Adampol, all by myself, not with the roads-- with the-- with the-- with the rye and the potatoes that was growing at that time. And I made it in the nighttime back to Adampol.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Adampol, I stayed there another week. And then the Germans just happened, they said that the Germans going to come and take out and kill the Jews there again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was-- it was a barn, where horses stayed. So I decided for that night to go in the hay, on top top of the barn. And some other people decided to go in the fields, in the rye. So, OK,

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there in that barn overnight. And in the morning, we went out. We saw 50-- 50 people got killed in the fields, in the rye.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was our last staying in Adampol. And from then on, we took off. It was not-- it's not far from there it was a-- it was the woods. They called it-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] what do call it? Ohojza. Ohojza meant that-- it was all around that woods was water.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was only a few dry spots there. So we went there, and we stayed a few days. And then the partisans-- people that already were there-- they came in, and they re-establshed a fire. And we went out-- we stole food from the-- from the villages. And we cooked there, and then we survived. And we stayed in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is Ohojza a whole area? The name of an area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's an  area. Only the woods-- the name, that place, called it Ohojza.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: As best you can.

speaker: interviewee
text: O-H-O-J-Z-A. Ohojza. That was it. It was-- the village near was Holesov. And it was-- the nearest-- the nearest-- the near village was [NON-ENGLISH]. That-- I remember that. So that was what the village is named.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the area that you lived in the woods for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't live there for too long. And we stayed there maybe two months. And then they were saying that there's going-- something happened there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody run away-- everybody run away from the woods. And I was-- and I didn't have to whom-- I didn't have no group to me, I was all alone. There was a few people like that-- all by themselves. And nobody wanted to have them, you know, because the groups went away. And they didn't want to be responsible for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, you know, there's a few Russian prisoners what had run away from the prison, came in the woods. And they said they're going Zerbugen-- they used to call it Zerbugen-- over the-- over the River Bug, on the other side of Bug. Because in the other side of Bug were real partisans, Russian partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they went there to join the real partisans. And they said, if we wanted, we can go with them. So we decided-- it was one, two, three, four, five-- I think five-- five guys of us went with them. So it took us two-- two days. We couldn't walk during the day, we walked at nighttime, you know. And I think after two days, we crossed over the Bug. And in a couple hours, we went in in a real Russian partisan [NON-ENGLISH]. And then we stayed in that place-- in that [NON-ENGLISH], we stayed in there two days. And somehow, they decided not to keep us there-- to send us deeper in Volyn. They are a lot of partisans-- the Jews, you know? And so they decided that we should go and join the other places there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave us a horse and wagon. And we traveled maybe-- maybe a week-- horse and wagon. And then we came in. That was near-- near Sarna-- the city, Sarna.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarna would be S-A-R-N-A-- Sarna. And that was in Volyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Volyn is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Volyn is the-- is the-- the whole-- the whole area is Volyn--call it Volyn. And then we stayed with that [NON-ENGLISH], I stayed there-- it was 19-- the whole year. And in 1944, when the Russians came a little nearer to Rovna and to Sarna, so we went out of the woods. And we helped the partisans defeat the Germans. In the woods, we were going to look out where Germans are. And the Germans were  already running back at that time. And it happened that in-- in I think in March, 1944, we met the Russian army at the city Klvin-- Klvin-- on the road to Klvin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-L-V-I-N. Klvin. Let it be that-- whoever knows. We met the Russian army in Klvin. So they went further down. And we went to Rovna. And that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-V-N-A? Rovna, R-O-V-A-N-- N-A, yes. And in Rovna, I stayed there with still with a partisan group, maybe a week or two weeks. And they let us go on our own. So I couldn't go back to Wlodawa, because at that time Wlodawa was-- the Germans were still there. So I went from Rovna to Sarna, and from Sarna to Rovna-- I was all by myself. I didn't know where to go and what to do. And no-- I'm not going to go to work, because nobody needs work at that time. Everybody wants to defeat the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember one time, I went to Rafalivka-- a little city-- the little village Rafalivka. And it was shul there. And we use to sleep at nighttime in the shul, you know. There was no house, you know, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Volyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Volyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Rafalivka. And it just happened in Rafalivka one time, the Russians came in. And they grabbed everybody into the army. And they took me, too, and they sent me away to Vologda.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell Vologda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vologda is Russian. Vologda with a V-O-L-O-G-D-A, Vologda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1944. I was already 19, right? 19, yeah. And I remember, I served in the Russian army for two months. And then, I am born in 1925. But when the Russians asked me what year I was born, I told them 1927. I don't know why I did it, but I did it. I didn't have-- I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: And exactly at that time, 1927, was not in the army yet, I was too young. So they let me go from the army. And they sent me over to Arkhangelsk to work there in a-- in a mill-- in a mill factory. You know mills-- for the wood. What do you call, is it mill? A wood mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: A wood mill, yes. And what I did there, I worked on the water, because they used to come with logs from far away, and with the water, to that mill. We had to take it apart, unchain it, and put it into the chains. The chains took it in, in the mill.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was my work. It was very hard work. It was very cold work. And the food wasn't-- not-- not for surviving. What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, come on. It was almost nothing-- a little piece of bread-- 600 grams of bread, you know, a day. And the soup was from the-- spinach-- not spinach, what they [NON-ENGLISH]. If you-- you became-- if you eat a leaf like that-- I don't know how to say it. [NON-ENGLISH]. You make some white pimples, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: You get a rash?

speaker: interviewee
text: A rash, right. And they used to cook that. And that was the most food what they gave us. We almost died there. So we decided, we cannot stay longer here, because we're going to die there anyways for hunger. And because before us was other people coming in there. And the girls used to tell us-- well, they used to take care in the houses, clean the houses there-- that every time, they bring in new people there, and-- and they never go home. They always die out there-- always die out there. We decided we were-- we were-- six boys, what they let us go from the army. We all wound up in Arkhangelsk. We decided that we have to go away from there-- that's going to be the end of us there. Of course, I was already weak, too. I just couldn't walk too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one-- one evening, we went on the train. And we decided to go back, because being in Arkhangelsk, I heard on the radio announcing, that my city, Wlodawa, was already taken over by the Russian army. So I wanted to go back to Wlodawa. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spring? Summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Summer. Yeah, summer. And so-- we were traveling all night long. But when we came from Arkhangelsk, we had to go to-- we took the route to Vologda. And all of a sudden, we were sitting in the-- in the toilet there. And we thought, nobody's going to bother us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we came near Vologda, somebody opens up the door. And he says, hey, [NON-ENGLISH], what are you doing here? I mean, comrades, what are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us off, and they took us to Vologda. And while we were going down, I think four guys disappeared. I with another one-- they kept us. The other four, we don't know what happened to them. Until today, I don't know what happened to them. They just-- while going down, somehow they turned away. And they-- and I never saw him, I never heard from him-- nothing. I mean it. That's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to Vologda, there to the police. And they told us-- we had some documents, and we were in the partisans. And they asked us, where are you traveling? We said-- told him, we want to go back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, the war is not over yet. When the war will be over, then you're going to go home, legal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So meanwhile, we had to find a place where to work. So we went on the bazaar there. And somebody gave us a place to go in [NON-ENGLISH], to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so we went in that [NON-ENGLISH], and we worked there until the war was over. When the war was over, they-- they allowed me to leave that place and going back to Wlodawa. And I traveled, of course, I traveled maybe a week  or whatever, maybe more. I don't remember how long. When I arrived to Wlodawa, coming back from there, I saw my aunt. But she was-- she survived in the woods, too-- my mother's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam. And her first husband got killed-- the Germans killed him. So she met a guy in the woods. They know-- they know-- they know each other before the war. So she survived, and he survived. And they took me in there, in their house. And I felt like I am my mother and father back there. And the state of Wlodawa-- I used to go from Wlodawa to Szczecin, drive back and forth, you know-- here and there. And I-- because I heard in Szczecin my aunt and uncle, what-- when the Russians came in 19-- 1939, they came to Wlodawa. And they said to the people that we should go-- everybody should go and leave Wlodawa, go with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my aunt and uncle, with my cousin-- they went there. And they survived-- they survived the war. And they went-- they came back from Russia after the war to Szczecin. So I saw-- I went to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went back, back and forth, until we decided we're going to leave Wlodawa for good. We left Wlodawa, with my uncle and my aunt. And I think my aunt had a girl born, and I helped her going-- we went to Szczecin, with the idea we're not going to stay in Szczecin with the idea that we were going to go to Germany. And from Germany, we're going to emigrate to some other country. That was what happened. And we stayed there a while in Szczecin. And later on, somebody-- I don't know, my uncle arranged it-- that they took us with a truck over the border, the Polish border. And we arrived in Berlin. So-- and I wind up in a DP camp in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schlachtensee. And I stayed there until the-- until the blockade-- in Schlachtensee until the blockade-- the Russians put the blockade on Berlin. So the Americans took out the camps from Schlachtensee. And they always settled them to first Germany. And I came to the-- and I came to a camp in West Germany named Wasseralfingen. And then from Wasseralfingen, they liquidated Wasseralfingen. And from Wasseralfingen-- while being in Wasseralfingen, somebody made a matchmaker for me. They know that the woman that I am married now to, that they know she's single and nice looking. And I was all alone, by myself, nobody. That I should see something. So I went-- she was-- she was living at that time in Windsheim. And I went and met her there. And I liked her. I don't know if she liked me, but somehow, we got engaged there. And then Windsheim was liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Windsheim was another DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then Windsheim was liquidated. And Wasseralfingen was liquidated. They sent us  in to Lechfeld. They sent us into Lechfeld. In Lechfeld, we got married there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop the tape.
